the exact information we're looking for.

What information are we looking for?
How do we determine what a good opportunity looks like?

Are we trying to help them measure so that we can ensure that when they go through an evaluation, you know, we can actually move it to a closed one rather than a test and then move on.
A big thing being, what if they go up this evaluation, they test the product, they just fix their issues, and from there never make a purchase.
So how can we continue to build value on the product and why they should continue to utilize it even after going through an evaluation?
So it was a big process.

They wear many hats.

We can move towards trying to close them immediately.

We have to actually get some code in there.

We can show samples of what the accessibility product looks like, but it's always better where you can give a more unique experience, one that's more customized and tailored to what they'll be working in or dealing with.
And so from there, we typically will do a trial.

don't need like handholding.

keep in touch.

um,

don't think we should charge so small amount.

I always find that Tyler's been trying that lately too, just like going up, just like quoting a little bit higher, just not even based on anything necessarily, just to test the elasticity of it at first.

Manager Manager
And at Contentful, she was very process oriented and very organized.
While I had my own process, I wouldn't say it was super refined.
And at the time, I probably wasn't the most organized rep either.

So those were conversations that we had to have a few times about how I could improve there, how I could better segment my time throughout the day and on my calendar, ways I could refine my process as well, and also how I could improve my creativity with how I was doing my outbound and how I could improve in that aspect.
She didn't have much to say in other areas that I was doing well in, but those were three things that she said kind of stood out where making that change could help take me to the next level.

And so from there, we kind of worked on ways I could implement that, know, blocking out time on my calendar, making sure I'm setting specific time segments for what I'm going to do, whether that's prospecting, research, if it's calls, if it's updating Salesforce, whatever it's going to be, making sure that I'm organized and I have all that time set aside.
Two, when it came to the creativity is, yes, I was doing some personalization and things like that in my emails, but how could I continue to improve upon that?
How could I do something differently than what everyone else is sending?
And from there, it was also making other changes.
While I was doing personalization, I started to dig a little deeper.
How could I touch more on how this is important to this particular person and their position and the outcome that they're expecting?
How could I do things like give them a visual of what the product looks like?
Like, you know, Contentful, we were originally reaching out to developers.
From there, we kind of shifted and did more with marketing instead.

That's something I kind of pushed on and said, hey, let's give them a way to actually kind of get a visual of what their workflow looked like, get them interested, want to have a conversation with us.

Something as simple as adding a small, I always pronounce this word wrong or right, don't know, gif or kif, of kind of what the workflow looks like and say, hey, this is how you could do this in Contentful, how easy it is compared to how you're doing it now.
And from there, we found a lot of success, me and my BDR at the time, and we actually shared that with the team.
know.

Like I said, just blocking off that time in different areas of the calendar, having specific purposes for it as well.
I think those are just ways that she really helped me kind of take that next step in the way I do.

was like, yeah, steep learning curve, right?

Like what do you think were the most important things for them to see to want to convert?
Yeah, a mix of things.
One, there has to be the ease of use, how easy it is to actually get this set up and started.
But kind of going more in depth into that and actually showing how it's done is something that helped improve it.
But also asking the right questions, understanding that these engineers, these developers, you know, they have different sprints and different projects that they're working on.

Let me make sure that when we're setting up this evaluation, this POC that we're going through, that we're not setting at a time in which resources are being pulled from all these different directions.
You we want to ensure that we've at least got, you know, three or four people that can hop in.
They can all evaluate at the same time.
They can pull their notes together rather than it all being segmented and having one or two people here, one or two people there.
And now this two-week evaluation is.
Extend it to four weeks, six weeks, you know, and from there it's hard to keep people aligned and actually interested in wanting to move forward.

So that was a big thing was just how I framed a lot of the questions I was asking and understanding more about their day-to-day and their work schedules and what they had going on, other projects and initiatives outside of this particular evaluation that was going on.
just a way that was very helpful and keeping people on track and of one mind as well when it came to getting this completed so we can move forward to the next step.
Is there anything else you didn't do or the company didn't do in the trial period that you think could have moved the needle?
Like if you were in charge of all sales, like what would you change?
Maybe something a little more like sandboxy, I would say.
The biggest thing at Cyprus was that they had to bring their code in.
And there are all kind of other problems that can arise with that.
There could be security issues, security assessments that come up.
actually had that come up where you had a month-long security assessment we had to do.
So that can cause issues.
And I think providing something that's more of a sandbox environment, saying, hey, log in here, because it is a cloud offering.
And so here we have a live preview.
You can get an idea of how it works.
You can see everything.
You don't have to bring your own code in.
We can get you started immediately.
And then from there, on the side, if they want to bring some of their code in, once they get approvals or if we have to do any security assessments, they can.
But it just gives them a way to kind of get started more quickly and keep them on task as well.
I think that was a big hiccup that we ran into was the fact that it required them to bring their own code in.
And it's the same thing I ran into at Twilio as well, the fact that they need a developer to actually build it out.
And it takes real working hours.
And are these free trials?
So the difference with Cyprus Cyprus is.
We have the Cypress app, which is completely free, but it's not in the cloud.
So they can run that on their own machines.
You know, they can use like Visual Studio Code, for example.
Oh, is it open source?
Yeah, that's right.
And then with the cloud, it's a bit different.
So we provide the enterprise plan for them.
gives them all the features, you know, all the governance that they need, where they can do all the testing and everything.
But, you the cloud is the paid version.

It gives you the login and you can see all the extra data and, you anything that leadership likes to see.

It was like, how did, in, like, in what way did Twilio shape you the most as a salesperson?
Obviously, they have like a well-known sales organization.
So I'm just curious, like, what you took away from that.
One, the technical aspect of it.
know, worked with an API.
I mean, that was rare that we sold that.
So now that was strictly APIs, you know, I felt that I had to be a little more technical and have a little more understanding of how API works.
And also there was a lot more multi-threading at Twilio.
I was working with all these different contacts, you marketing was pretty much always involved because they were using the tools.
But we have to have a developer, you know, sometimes they're engineers.
You know, there's a lot more legal as well that's coming in, a lot of security assessments.
So now we got the cybersecurity team.
So there were so many different areas that I was working in and then partners were huge as well.
You know, there were companies that couldn't build their own solutions.
We worked with partners to bring them in.
So there was a lot more quarterbacking and trying to kind of get up to speed.
You know, I think it helped me learn how to acquire the knowledge quickly and get onboarded really fast so that I could ramp quickly and get straight to being successful.
And, you know, I think that kind of proves it, you know, at next two companies in which, you know, was pretty much.
Yeah.
Tell me about Contentful.
Like what, what shaped you there?
Like what, what did you really take away from that into Cypress?
If anything, I know it's very different market, like marketer, but like, like, yeah.
So what, what, what will you bring into your next thing, I guess, out of Contentful experience?
Yeah.
At Contentful, I got there with a lot more larger companies.
And so a lot more rigid processes, know, making sure that I knew what this was going to look like from start to finish.

Like I said, introducing those mutual success plans, mutual action plans, helping keeping everyone online a lot more, not just that quarterbacking, but like kind of like a game manager that we would look like, you know, for football as an example.
Yeah.

But, you know, having all these pieces where, you know, I'm thinking of myself as the head and I'm making sure they're all arranged in these right areas and that we're all moving towards the same point.
And so I think there was a lot more of that at Contentful just because the deals were larger, the companies were larger.

A lot more stakes and different stakes involved for different personas and what they were looking for, like what the CTO is interested in is much different than what the CMO is interested in, you know, and helping keeping those different personas happy and making sure I'm speaking to the value that speaks to them personally and what they're looking for out of the solution.

So I think it helped me kind of dig a little deeper as well, just because the solution in general, you think content and some people think, oh, that's, that's simple, but no, it expands to so many other different facets, you know, not just a website.

Like, you know, we're talking a mobile app, social media, all these different areas that you can produce digital content and really helping CMOs and others in those positions to think about strategy around that and what that looks like and how they could utilize Contentful to not only do what they're asking, but also to expand upon that to other areas they may not have thought of.
Yeah, that's, we're launching a platform probably in like two weeks.
We're launching two different, because we're, largely today it looks like services, but like we're, that's not how we want to be seen anymore.
Like we're launching two different platforms, like one in December and one probably in Q1 sometime, which will be our like core platform.
But one of them will be, like the first one is going to be like an LLM visibility tool.

So you can like see how your brand ranks in LLM search and different prompts you rank for and then under, then from there you can like build a content strategy off that of like, oh, people who look for expense, finance expense management tools are like prompting with these questions.
Therefore, like we want to make sure we have a lot of content centered around that.
So that's like one of the platforms will be like a visibility tool that will drive the strategy for either like our services team, which will continue to exist, like we'll always continue to do like.
Like a white glove offering of the platform for a premium.
Or we're going to launch like a self-serve version of the platform.
I mean, it doesn't need to be like fully self-serve.

So then it's like taking the strategy and the content ideas and then they should be able to actually like generate it themselves in the tool and edit it themselves in the tool.
So it'll be a bit more like end to end.
So like we're really going to try to become like a platform company in the next year.
I'm sure Tyler may be explaining this to you, but like a lot of the, like the sales experience will, will change a lot because I mean, while we will still always have, you know.
Okay.
If you don't want to operate any of this yourself and you want someone to completely outsource it like an agency, we can still do these like service deals.
But, um, we really want to like.
like,
Transform into a platform company.

can make your own account.

easier if the content strategy was pushed.
